summary: I was born and raised in Chicago, Illinois. My father worked in retail here, rising to a high position at Wieboldt's, a major department store chain in the Chicago metro area. I went to school in Chicago until my father bought a store in Wisconsin Dells in 1948, which led us to move. Chicago was where my early life and education began before the big change.

My father started his retail career at Wieboldt's, a major department store chain in Chicago with about seven or eight locations. He quickly rose to the number two position there, which showed his talent and dedication in retail. Wieboldt's was where he really got retail in his blood before moving on to other chains and eventually managing his own store.

After Wieboldt's, my father became a store manager for Spiegel's, which focused on house furniture and appliances. While working there, he was contacted about buying a small department store in Wisconsin Dells. Spiegel's was an important step in his career before he ventured into owning his own business.
cues: contacted about business in Wisconsin Dells

summary: In 1948, my family moved to Wisconsin Dells when my father bought a small department store there. The Dells is a vacation destination mainly for Chicagoans, with a huge influx of about 250,000 people from Memorial Day to Labor Day, but it drops to a small population of around 2,000 in the off-season. It was a big change from Chicago, and the town had mostly Swedish and German residents, plus a Native American tribe called the Winnebagos.
cues: 250,000 people Memorial Day to Labor Day

summary: Memorial Day marked the beginning of the busy season in Wisconsin Dells, when the population would swell to around 250,000 visitors. This influx lasted until Labor Day, making it a critical period for my father's department store business. Outside of these months, the town's population was very small, which made running a year-round business challenging.
cues: Memorial Day start of busy season

summary: Labor Day marked the end of the tourist season in Wisconsin Dells, after which the population dropped back to about 2,000 residents. This seasonal shift made it tough to maintain business year-round, as most visitors left for warmer places like Florida, California, and Arizona. My father's store had to adapt to this cycle to survive.
cues: Labor Day end of tourist season
people go to Florida, California, Arizona

summary: The Winnebago people were the Native American tribe present in Wisconsin Dells, where we moved in 1948. They were one of the few ethnic groups in the area alongside Swedish, German, and one Jewish family. Their presence was part of the unique cultural makeup of the town, which had very few outsiders like us Italians.

My father's department store in Wisconsin Dells was like a small Macy's, with a wide variety of goods including clothes, dry goods, blankets, groceries, and shoes. It was a two-story building but all retail was on the first floor. Macy's was the model I would picture when thinking about the kind of store he ran, just on a smaller scale.

In 1948, my father bought a small department store in Wisconsin Dells and renamed it Cappy's Department Store. He changed the family name from Capizzi to Cappy for business reasons, wanting something that sounded more professional and was easier to announce in a large store. The store carried a wide range of dry goods and household items but no furniture or appliances.
cues: name change from Capizzi to Cappy

summary: The Great Depression, starting with the 1929 Wall Street crash, was a very difficult time for my family and many others. My parents lived through it and taught me that you only bought what you could pay for in cash—there was no credit to rely on. It shaped how they managed money and influenced the way they raised me.

My father's family originally came from Sicily. His father was a mason and did various types of manual work there, but he wanted better opportunities. So around the late 1800s or just before 1900, the family emigrated from Sicily to America seeking a better life.

My grandfather and his family arrived in America through Ellis Island around 1900. They couldn't speak or read English when they came, but they had letters from others who had already immigrated. Ellis Island was the gateway for their new life in the United States.

Many people from Wisconsin Dells, including some from my family, would go to Florida during the winter to stay warm. After the busy summer season ended around Labor Day, the town's population dropped as people left for warmer climates like Florida. My siblings now live in Tampa, Florida.

California was one of the warm places people from Wisconsin Dells would go to during the winter months. After the summer tourist season ended, many residents left for places like California to avoid the cold. It was part of the seasonal migration pattern that affected the local economy.

Arizona was another warm destination for people leaving Wisconsin Dells in the winter. Like Florida and California, it attracted those wanting to escape the cold after the busy summer season. This seasonal movement was something my family and community experienced regularly.
